data.title: Adobe: Fighting Generative AI With Share Repurchases
data.description:
Generative AI has created its share of winners and losers. But what about the stocks in between? Adobe Inc. (ADBE) continues to churn out respectable top-line growth backed by best-in-class profit margins. But investors appear worried that headwinds from generative AI are around the corner and hard to shake off. The company has been aggressively repurchasing stock, even allowing its net cash position to dissipate. The stock valuation looks attractive, allowing for solid returns even if top-line growth decelerates meaningfully. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

ADBE Stock Price

I last covered ADBE in August, where I rated the stock a Strong Buy as one of the “last few buys in software.” The stock has declined by around 6% since then.

There still is no visible catalyst to overhang the generative AI overhang, but I see more upside ahead.

ADBE Stock Key Metrics

ADBE is an enterprise software juggernaut known for products like Photoshop and Illustrator. Generative AI and its image-generation capabilities have cast doubt on the future of ADBE’s relevance, leading to a dramatic re-rating downwards. That, however, has not stopped the company from posting strong results. In the most recent quarter, the company delivered 11% YoY revenue growth to $5.99 billion, exceeding guidance of between $5.875 billion and $5.925 billion. Non-GAAP EPS of $5.31 exceeded guidance of between $5.15 and $5.20.

Current remaining performance obligations ("cRPOs") grew 13% YoY, possibly suggesting that there is no sudden drop-off in revenues expected in the near term.

The company ended the quarter with $5.94 billion in cash versus $6.2 billion in debt. The company has reduced its net cash position due to aggressive share repurchases.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see 9.2% YoY growth in revenue to $6.125 billion. As usual, I expect guidance to prove at least slightly conservative.

On the conference call, management highlighted that their “AI-influenced ARR has now surpassed $5 billion, up from over $3.5 billion exiting fiscal year 2024” and that this has already surpassed their prior full-year target. I suspect that this disclosure of AI revenues has not quelled investor concerns about potential disruption from generative AI, as investors may have been hoping for strong AI revenue growth to lead to an acceleration in overall revenue growth (which has not come to fruition). I note that management was asked regarding their confidence in being able to sustain double-digit growth, to which they answered that they are “not providing targets for next year.” I interpreted their response to indicate that investors may need to prepare for the likelihood that revenue growth ends up going lower and not higher.

Is ADBE Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, ADBE traded hands at just around 14x forward earnings. That is a steep discount to the 5-year average valuation of around 32x earnings.

Shares were selling for around 5.4x forward sales, also representing a large discount to the 5-year average valuation of around 12x sales. Consensus estimates are already factoring in a dip below a double-digit top-line growth rate.

The re-rating lower makes sense given both the deceleration in growth rates as well as the deterioration in the growth story. Whereas ADBE was once viewed as a rare tech stock that could balance both aggressive top-line growth and robust profit margins, it is now facing threats from generative AI and competitors. That said, I am of the view that the re-rating is overdone. The company still maintains a fairly conservative balance sheet, and all cash flow is going towards share repurchases, taking advantage of the low stock valuation.

While I expect some disruption to the smaller customer base, I expect the vast majority of ADBE’s business to remain resilient as customers are likely to continue wanting best-in-class software tools. I note that ADBE may be able to offset some of the headwinds through continued uptake of its AI and agentic tools. Given the high profit margins, the stock looks attractive to me even if top-line growth decelerates to the 4% to 6% range. I can see the stock re-rating to around 20x earnings over time, at the very least. Between the 7% earnings yield and top line, I can see this stock delivering 10% to 13% annual returns based on these conservative assumptions. The key here is to keep expectations moderate (something that I may not have done a good enough job of in the past), as shaking the generative AI risk overhang may not be so easy here.

ADBE Stock Risks

ADBE is facing potentially disruptive threats from many sources. Figma (FIG) continues to post strong growth and, from the outside looking in, appears to be at the very least highly competitive in their direct competition. Generative AI might enable customers to reduce their use of design-editing tools. Generative AI might help competitive products narrow the competitive gap. Generative AI might lead to mass layoffs, which would hurt seat-based vendors like ADBE.

ADBE Stock Conclusion

ADBE is not as exciting as fast-growing AI stocks, but the cash cow is happily consuming the deeply discounted shares. I expect the impact from generative AI and competition to prove more moderate than feared. The stock is already pricing in pessimistic scenarios, and I expect the stock to benefit from some multiple expansion. ADBE is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Docebo Continues Downward Revenue Growth Trend (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Docebo Inc. (DCBO) recently reported its Q3 2025 financial results, beating consensus estimates for both top line revenue and earnings.

I previously analyzed DCBO in March 2025, downgrading the stock to a Hold outlook due to a dimming revenue growth outlook.

I’m further downgrading DCBO to a Sell on a continued lower revenue growth rate, no meaningful upside catalysts in the near term and unpredictable results from its U.S. federal market efforts.

Docebo’s Market And Approach

Docebo is a pure play provider of corporate learning management systems.

According to a 2025 Grand View Research market research report by Grand View Research, the global market for e-learning was approximately $300 billion in 2024 and is expected to reach $842 billion by 2030.

If accomplished, this growth rate would be a CAGR of around 19% from 2025 to 2030, which is a very strong expected growth rate.

The primary drivers for this high forecasted growth rate are an increasing need for employee and student upskilling, hybrid work arrangements at a meaningful percentage of organizations and the ongoing requirement to improve cost efficiencies.

The chart below shows the expected growth path of the worldwide E-learning market through 2030 and by region:

A number of competitive e-learning industry players are listed here:

  • Instructure

  • McGraw Hill (MH)

  • Absorb LMS

  • Udemy (UDMY)

  • Coursera (COUR)

  • Saba Cloud

  • SAP SuccessFactors Learning (SAP)

  • Udacity

  • Tovuti LMS

  • 360Learning

  • Cornerstone OnDemand

  • SumTotal Learning

  • Captivate Prime.

Docebo derives 71% of its total revenue from the United States and almost all of its revenue by type from subscriptions, as the pie charts below indicate:

Docebo’s go-to-market efforts are primarily led by its direct sales approach, but it also has partnerships with integration partners such as Workday (WDAY) and via channel partners like Vartopia and Bluewater.

Also, it targets users directly via its online platform with scalable pricing tiers to meet customer demand, depending on size and usage.

DCBO’s nearest competitor is likely Instructure, which was acquired by KKR (KKR) in November 2024 for an EV of $4.8 billion, a 16% premium to its pre-announcement enterprise value.

Industry trends in the corporate LMS space include integrating AI and automation-enhanced features to help companies improve their employee skills, improved analytics for business impact measurement and mobile-first/remote worker learning environments that can be accessed anywhere and anytime.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary market characteristics:

Strengths

  • Strong position in the corporate LMS market, revenue stability from subscription model, worldwide footprint, emphasis on AI features and modern learning modes.

Weaknesses

  • Sliding growth rate, smaller scale than other players, dependence on retention and expansion of existing customers.

Opportunities

  • Expansion into new verticals (government), geographic expansion, increasing demand for external training.

Threats

  • Intense competition from many LMS providers, need for continued R&D enhancements, slower enterprise sales cycles on larger prospects.

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen due to mid-market outperformance and EMEA region strength. Operating income by quarter (red line) has improved because of greater SG&A leverage.

The degree of operating leverage has turned down from my previous Q4 2024 report, primarily from a Q1 2025 negative result; otherwise, it has improved in the most recent quarter:

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has moved lower as a result of a mix shift toward professional services and onboarding costs. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have dropped and management is expecting a further decline on greater efficiencies. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have reflected continued investment in its generative AI integration efforts.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have bounced back from an early 2025 plunge.

(All data in the above charts is IFRS.)

For balance sheet results, DCBO ended the quarter with $66.1 million in cash and equivalents and no debt.

In the past 12 months, DCBO’s stock price has fallen by a hefty 55% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Expanded Technology-Software ETF’s (IGV) rise of 3%, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a helpful financial and operating metrics table for Docebo that includes both trailing twelve-month and forward expected figures:

The company's Rule of 40 performance has been trailing unevenly downward in recent quarters, as the chart shows here:

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was a reasonably robust $30.3 million, and capital expenditures were a very low $1.1 million.

The company paid $6.1 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was $24.2 million.

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at a hefty $44.4 million, indicating the company is retiring stock at a good rate.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 2.6%, a fairly low figure for a company like Docebo.

The company continues to invest in its government efforts and platform enhancements.

Overall, Docebo’s capital allocation is shareholder-friendly on disciplined SBC, minimal CapEx and strong stock buybacks.

Why I’m Bearish On Docebo

In the lengthening aftermath of the pandemic period stock price runup due to that period’s sharp growth, Docebo’s stock has performed disappointingly, as the firm’s growth rates have trended lower over time.

While the industry has seen recent developments with AI-enhanced learning feature growth, the result has been a need for dedicating R&D spend on those enhancements.

In the end, it’s difficult to see major differentiation from those changes, at least among the more forward-looking vendors in the LMS space.

Management’s current growth strategy involves continued focus on middle-market opportunities, growth in U.S. federal and SLED as a result of its FedRAMP certification, and AI-enhanced product features that improve product differentiation and upsell impetus.

However, U.S. federal contract progress may be slowed by volatility in administration priorities, although the company says that the recent shutdown hasn’t materially affected its pipeline progress.

The potential benefit of its U.S. federal efforts is that once it gains acceptance and use in a particular department, it is more difficult to dislodge, although revenue wins may be lumpy from contract wins or losses.

I’ve prepared a chart showing the frequency of negative-oriented keyword mentions in the most recent conference call of management with analysts:

The graphic illustrates that the company is continuing to see "deal elongation" in the commercial market, as companies exert deal scrutiny in an uncertain macroeconomic environment.

Docebo also saw a greater Dayforce white label/OEM relationship wind down effect on revenue, which should continue through 2026 but become immaterial in 2027.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing DCBO at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 2.5x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of 12.9% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. DCBO not providing this important figure.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 4.9x on October 31, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, DCBO is currently being valued by public investors at a significant discount to the broader index as of October 31, 2025.

Looking ahead, management is guiding to a topline revenue growth rate of 11.4% in 2025, significantly lower than in past years.

It is difficult to see a meaningful catalyst for the stock in the near term, since topline revenue growth is slowing from its Dayforce wind down and other market factors.

While the corporate e-learning market is expected to see robust growth in the coming years, DCBO appears to be losing market share due to its lower comparative growth rate.

It may gain some U.S. federal business, but even that is lumpy and difficult to predict.

As such, my estimation is that investors may be able to earn better rates of return on their capital elsewhere, so I’m downgrading DCBO further to a Sell.

Market Review

The global economic landscape remained broadly stable through the third quarter, though underlying fragilities persisted. Most major central banks held interest rates steady, with the exception of the Federal Reserve, which delivered a widely anticipated 25bp cut and the Bank of England, which also cut rates by 25bp. Inflation trends varied: modest increases were seen in the US, UK, and China, while Japan experienced a gradual easing. Growth data across developed markets showed modest expansion, though generally below long-term trends, and business confidence indicators remained mixed. Labor markets, particularly in the US, showed signs of softening, while fiscal pressures intensified in Europe, culminating in the collapse of the French government over budget disagreements.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the Fund’s Class I shares (SDGIX) returned 1.24%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate USD Hedged Index, returned 1.21% for the same period.

Market Review (continued)

Trade tensions escalated as the US resumed tariff hikes following the expiry of a 90-day pause, imposing steep duties on goods from China, Canada, Mexico, Switzerland, and threatening India over Russian oil imports. These measures injected uncertainty into global trade flows. Geopolitical tensions remained elevated but less dominant, with a brief and inconclusive meeting between Presidents Trump and Putin in Alaska regarding the Ukraine conflict. Overall, while global growth held steady, inflation, trade policy unpredictability, and political instability continued to weigh on the outlook heading into the final quarter of the year.

There was some divergence across government bond markets in the third quarter. Despite concerns about fiscal expansion, the Federal Reserve eventually cut interest rates amid signs of a softening economy and weaker labor market conditions. US yields were lower with the 10-year Treasury yields declining 8 basis points (bp) to 4.15%. Elsewhere, government bond markets were generally weaker as yields rose. In Germany yields trended upwards, although not uniformly, with 10-year yields ending 11bp higher at 2.71%. Meanwhile, 10-year UK gilt yields rose more substantially as concerns about debt levels and the sustainability of the deficit increased, reaching 4.70%, an increase of 22bp. In Japan, government bond yields also increased. The 10-year yield exceeded 1.6% for the first time since the global financial crisis, rising 22bp to 1.65%. With US Treasury yields having fallen, yield levels in the emerging markets also declined, with the JP Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index ending 8bp lower at 5.93%.

Credit markets continued to make gains relative to governments as spreads tightened towards new multi-year lows through the quarter, despite an apparent lack of much positive global economic momentum. Both investment grade and high yield markets outperformed governments. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) over governments for the Bloomberg US Corporate Index ended the quarter 9bp tighter at 74bp, with the related Bloomberg US Credit Index also 9bp tighter at 70bp. The spread on the Bloomberg Euro Corporate Index also tightened by 13bp at 79bp. The Bloomberg Sterling Agg Corporate Index was 15bp tighter at 86bp. In high yield markets, the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index spread was 23bp tighter at 267bp, while the Bloomberg Pan-European High Yield Index spread tightened by 38bp to 265bp.

Performance Review

Duration was a tailwind on the quarter, with an overweight to the US being a driver of the positive return. Curve positioning yielded additional positive alpha with strong performance from a Japanese flattener and UK flatteners more than compensating the negative performance of a US steepener. Cross-market trades also outperformed, spurred by overweights in New Zealand versus Australia and Canada versus the US, but an overweight in the UK versus the US detracted. Active positioning in US Agency MBS outperformed. On the quarter, the primary headwind to performance was our underweight in credit beta, while our preference for euro denominated investment grade credit versus its US dollar counterpart contributed. Active foreign currency positioning yielded a positive contribution to performance, with an underweight in New Zealand dollar and Swiss franc being its primary catalysts. Active inflation positioning yielded additional if modest alpha.

Market Outlook

The global growth outlook remains underwhelming, with expansion rates generally in the 1% to 2% range in the major developed market economies, which is almost universally a sub-par rate. Elsewhere, China is struggling to achieve the target growth rate it sets for itself. Inflation is expected to remain sticky and above central bank target levels in some places. Further rate cuts are expected to be dependent on inflation expectations and whether labour markets slacken or remain relatively robust. The primary wildcard is the effect that US trade tariffs will have on growth and inflation. We believe the US Federal Reserve will ease policy further before reaching a neutral rate, with 2026 facing the uncertainty of who is appointed the new Fed Chair. The Bank of England is likely to ease rates once more, while the ECB may have completed its policy easing for this cycle. The Bank of Japan faces entrenched inflationary pressure and is expected to tighten policy further, albeit gradually. Political uncertainty remains elevated with leadership elections in Japan, ineffectiveness in France and the shutdown in the US. In the US, some macro indicators suggest there is resilience within the economy, but other factors such as the softening labour market align more with further slowdown. While we continue to believe that a recession is unlikely, it does remain a clear risk if the effects of tariffs and immigration crackdowns squeeze demand further. We anticipate GDP growth of 1.6% for 2025 as a whole, with similar sub-trend activity again in 2026. Inflation is still above the Fed’s target and the effects of tariffs are likely to maintain upward pressure on prices if businesses do not or cannot absorb them. However, the weaker labour market may help prevent an unruly escalation in inflation levels. Having eventually resumed its easing cycle after nine months, the Fed appears to be turning its attention as much to the labour market as to tariffs, and we see further cuts to come that may take the Fed funds rate to the 3.25% to 3.5% range. Treasury yields are expected to ease, with the 10-year yield declining below 4%, towards 3.85% in 12-months’ time. The US government shutdown will potentially lead to weaker sentiment and, until resolved, reduce the availability of data produced at a federal level.

In the eurozone, the manufacturing sector, which has been under pressure for some time, remains weak. Positive real wage growth in 2025 could support consumption, but subdued consumer confidence offers little cause for optimism the consumption will accelerate. On the positive side, the prospect of a large German fiscal package adds some upside risks to our growth forecast, while the US tariffs add significant downside risk via both the trade and uncertainty channels. Eurozone inflation has been around 2% for some time but we forecast it will modestly fall next year owing to base effects. The effect of tariffs will be nuanced; any retaliatory tariff price hikes may be tempered by softer demand and China could look to Europe as an alternative market. We do not expect further rate cuts in 2025, though there may be one more cut in 2026. Bond yields are not expected to diverge greatly from current levels for the time being. Politically, in France, difficulties in forming a government continue. As we expect little progress in the near term given the polarised nature of the French parliament, the situation leaves political risk elevated.

In the emerging markets, China’s economy appears to be stabilising but ongoing domestic issues, notably in the property sector, remain a hurdle to achieving the growth trajectory the authorities would like. Tariffs and global trade tensions are combining with the underlying demographic factors, such that we believe the 5% target for GDP growth will be missed both this year and in 2026. Given the subdued growth outlook, we expect inflation to remain low for some time. The Chinese authorities are expected to intensify fiscal and monetary support, aiming to stimulate domestic demand and the property sector. Any interest rate cuts that the People’s Bank of China implements will likely be only modest. GDP growth rates in other emerging market countries are relatively robust, but many still face the effects of US tariffs with little prospect of them being lifted soon. As with most developed market economies, inflation is expected to continue moderating slowly over time, though there may be volatility in that process. Interest rate cuts may also be forthcoming, particularly if the US continues to give ground and the Fed cuts rates further.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries. High yield bonds involve increased credit and liquidity risk than higher-rated bonds and are considered speculative in terms of the issuer's ability to pay interest and repay principal on a timely basis. The use of derivatives involves risks different from, or possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in the underlying assets. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid, and difficult to value and there is the risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the portfolio will not correlate with the underlying instruments or the portfolio's other investments.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (Unhedged) is a flagship measure of global investment grade debt from twenty- four local currency markets. This multi-currency benchmark includes treasury, government-related, corporate and securitized fixed-rate bonds from both developed and emerging markets issuers. J.P. Morgan Market Bond indices are a family of benchmarks designed to track the performance of various bond markets, including those in the US, emerging markets, and global markets. These indices cover a range of asset classes and currencies, providing investors with a way to track and compare the performance of different bond sectors. The Bloomberg US Corporate Index is a benchmark for the investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable US corporate bond market. It includes USD-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers. The Bloomberg Euro Corporate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that tracks the performance of the investment-grade, euro-denominated, fixed-rate corporate bond market. It includes securities from industrial, utility, and financial issuers publicly issued in Eurobond and eurozone domestic markets. The Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index measures the USD-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. The Bloomberg Sterling Aggregate Corporate Bond Index is a fixed-rate, investment-grade Sterling-denominated corporate bond index that tracks the performance of the fixed-rate, investment-grade Sterling-denominated corporate bond market. It's part of a broader family of fixed income indices offered by Bloomberg. The index includes bonds from industrial, utility, and financial issuers. The Bloomberg Pan-European High Yield Index tracks the performance of fixed-rate, high- yield corporate bonds issued in Europe, denominated in EUR, GBP, CHF, DKK, NOK, and SEK. It specifically focuses on non-investment grade debt, excluding emerging market debt and certain other debt types. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS) is an investment similar to a bond that is made up of a bundle of home loans bought from the banks that issued them. Investors in MBS receive periodic payments similar to bond coupon payments. A yield curve is a line that plots yields, or interest rates, of bonds that have equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country’s borders in a specific time period. Inflation is the rate of increase in the cost of living. Inflation is usually quoted as an annual percentage, comparing the average price this month with the same month a year earlier. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) is the measurement of the spread of a fixed-income security rate and the risk-free rate of return, which is then adjusted to take into account an embedded option. A basis point (BP) is a unit of measure used to indicate percentage changes in financial instruments. Basis points are typically expressed with the abbreviations "bp," "bps," or "bips." One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%. In decimal form, one basis point appears as 0.0001 (0.01/100).

data.title: CoreWeave: This Isn't The Bubble Pop, Yet
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) stock has cratered post Q3 earnings, down 24% over the past five days, adding to last week’s bloodbath and bringing the stock down 42% over the past month. Needless to say, markets have been feeling the heat, and CoreWeave hasn't been an easy name to own.

This comes after CoreWeave had already underperformed the market during 3QCY25, declining 16% versus the S&P 500, up 8%. Now, CoreWeave is down 43% since our last article in early October titled “Something is Very Wrong.” The stock was priced for perfection, and any slip-up would’ve brought the market down crashing, and there was no shortage of triggers, including Michael Burry’s bearish take on Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir, the government shutdown, and angsty reaction to tier 1 raised CapEx and OpenAI (OPENAI) committing $1.4T in AI infrastructure deals.

This quarter, CoreWeave bit the bullet: management trimmed FY25 outlook to $5.05B-$5.15B from $5.15B-$5.35B, trailing estimates for $5.29B, and cut CapEx to $12B-$14B, substantially lower than $20B-$23B previously expected. CoreWeave said the reason for the downward revisions is:

temporary delays related to a third-party data center developer behind schedule.

The customer affected by the delays is willing to wait it out, so there is no hit to the total value of the original contract, but everything is pushed out into FY26. Q4 CapEx is now expected to fall into Q1, with management eyeing higher CapEx, at least double, in 2026, stating that

“given the significant growth in our backlog and continued insatiable demand for our cloud services, we expect CapEx in 2026 to be well in excess of double that of 2025.”

What does Q3 tell us?

This isn’t CoreWeave's first post-earnings crash; last quarter, the stock also took a nosedive after the print due to the higher-than-expected quarterly loss and operating expenses jumping 275% from a year ago quarter.

For Q3, operating expenses were $1,312,826, higher than last quarter’s $1,193,579 by 10%. Revenue, similarly to Q2, grew triple-digits Y/Y to revenue of $1.36B, ahead of the expected $1.29B and loss of $0.22 per share. The company ended the quarter with a backlog of $55.6B, adding about $25B during the quarter largely due to the deal with Meta Platforms (META) and OpenAI. The problem for investors, aside from the stock being priced for perfection, is the fact that in spite of such a hefty backlog, management is cutting FY25 guidance, and keep in mind we still have little visibility on the 2026 growth outlook.

To us, the Q4 outlook and the lowered guide for FY25 are less reflective of a bubble pop moment and more reflective of a new product ramp that comes with a headache.

Investors should remember that a big part of why CoreWeave became Wall Street’s darling so quickly is its cozy relationship with Nvidia and the latter’s 7% stake, and that gets CoreWeave to be first in line for Nvidia’s next B300/GB300 product line, shown below. The ramp of B300/GB300 is trickier than markets are expecting because it is all new, and the industry is still figuring out how to make the tech itself work without overheating or liquid-cooling leaks. So, we think this is why CoreWeave took such a financial hit this quarter; the products aren’t ready due to delays in the supply chain, and so the financials are lagging behind.

Our longer-term concern for CoreWeave is that its business model is inherently flawed, because it'll require continued high upfront CapEx to offset the risk of tech obsolescence. We get into this in more depth here on our post-IPO thoughts on CoreWeave's business. This is not the reason we believe the stock is crashing now; instead, we view this sell-off as market panic over the impact of delays amid heightened AI bubble concerns. There will be a time to run from CoreWeave, but we don’t think this is it yet.

The company still has an active power of 590 MW, with the goal of 850 MW of active power by year’s end. The total power with contracted capacity is over 2.9 GW, which makes CoreWeave an industry pick for capacity lease agreements, particularly from enterprise and hyperscalers. The company has +1 GW of contracted capacity available to come online within the upcoming 12-24 months. Remember that big picture, CoreWeave is still the only neocloud with a Platinum ranking from SemiAnalysis. The fact that we’re also seeing CoreWeave’s customer concentration issue ease is a positive signal for us, given that, as of Q3, no single data center provider accounted for more than 20% of contracted power.

Bottom Line:

CoreWeave stock has shed over 40% of its value over the past month. RSI is deepening into oversold territory currently at 23, as shown below. Valuation has come down slightly, although the stock continues to trade at an AI premium. Forward EV/Sales is down from 16.25x in early October to 11.18x. We believe the stock has priced in the short-term negatives and see outperformance in 1H26 as AI-driven anxiety eases and the B300/GB300 ramp turns more successful.

The stock is trading at $77 per share at current levels, down from its high of $187 per share and CoreWeave is the worst performing neocloud on the YTD, 6M, 3M and 1M chart. The chart below is CoreWeave's six-month chart.

We think the reality is that CoreWeave is a high-risk, high-reward pick. Investors are circulating more money into newer bitcoin-mining turned AI infrastructure plays, and confidence in CoreWeave is wanning.

At current values, we think the bulk of the negatives have been priced into CoreWeave's stock price and outlook for FY25. A lot weighs on the broader AI narrative and continued concern over overspend without guaranteed return on investment. We say the bulk of the negatives, because we expect Nvidia's Q3 earnings report next week (November 19) to potentially weigh on expectations due to potential delay issues with the B300/GB300 ramp. We believe what markets are now digesting is that CoreWeave's positioning is a double-edged sword, and we see room for outperformance as expectations reset.

data.title: S&P 500: Bears Emphasize Record P/E, But Overlook Record Profit Margins
data.description:
Bears love emphasizing record P/E ratios

As major indices (including DJI, SP500, and NASDAQ) are all hovering around all-time-high prices, the elevated valuation metrics of the overall equity market have become the focus of many market bears’ critique. Take the S&P 500 index as an example; its current Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio sits near 31x, as you can see from the following chart. This level is not only significantly higher than long-term historical averages (or around 15x), but also among the most expensive levels in the index’s entire history. In the past 5 decades, the P/E exceeded this level only 3~4 times, depending on how you count. Furthermore, the extreme P/E observed during the 2008 crisis and 2020 recession was caused by temporary earnings decline. Based on such elevated P/E, the bears then go on to argue the market is dangerously detached from fundamental reality.

Against this background, the goal of this article is to offer a counterargument. In the remaining sections of this article, I will argue that this focus on P/E overlooks the profit margins of the index, which are also near record levels in the index’s history. I will further explain why I see good reasons for the index’s profit margin to remain robust for the years to come. Given the higher margin—and thus higher quality of the earnings—I believe the current valuation risks in the equity market is far less precarious than it appears.

But S&P 500 profit margins are also near record levels

The next chart shows the profit margin of the S&P 500 index in the past 5 decades, dating back to the 1970s. As seen, the current profit margin, last recorded at 10.905% in October 2025, is substantially higher than the long-term historical average of 6.309% and almost double the median of 5.678% in this period. The current reading is also among the peak levels in the index’s entire history, only slightly below the temporary peak of 12.629% observed in December 2021.

So, what are the factors that have driven up the SP500’s secular expansion of profit margins? Among the many driving forces economists have hypothesized and debated, two stand out, and both are likely to persist into the future in my model, as detailed next.

Why high margins could continue

First, corporate tax rates have been drastically lowered over the years, as you can clearly see from the following chart. Based on data from the Peterson Foundation, due to the lower tax rates, the United States now collects fewer revenues from corporations, measured as a percentage of its GDP, than most similarly developed countries. In 2022, for example, the latest year for which data is available for international comparison, U.S. corporate tax revenues accounted for just 1.3 percent of GDP, while the average fraction is 3.2% among the 13 developed countries.

Given the prevailing political climate, I see little odds for corporate tax rates to increase in any material way. And as a true believer in the free market, I see a lower corporate rate as a structural advantage for the equity market. Everything I’ve learned and experienced has made me believe it is far more efficient for individual corporations to allocate resources for themselves than for a central government to allocate on their behalf.

Second, I believe the US economy has also undergone a structural shift to asset-light technology and information services over the years. As a reflection, the composition of the S&P 500 index has shifted away from capital-intensive manufacturing and retail toward asset-light, high-margin sectors like Information Technology and Communication Services. These modern firms, such as the MAG 7, which now dominate the index's market capitalization, rely heavily on intellectual property, software, and network effects rather than physical assets. This business model allows for extreme economies of scale (where the cost of serving an additional customer is near zero) and also rapid penetration to markets outside the US. You can see from the next chart below that the Information Technology sector reports margins soaring toward ~26.8%, both above its own historical average and also more than double the index’s average margin.

With the infrastructure and also technologies invested by the sector over the years, I see the scalability of these asset-light businesses to keep driving robust growth while maintaining (even further expanding) profit margins.

Other risks and final thoughts

There are a couple of places where my above analysis could go wrong. First and foremost, another key driver for the margin expansion in the past is the integration of global supply chains. This crucial tailwind has been helping the long-term relative suppression of labor costs and raw material costs for US corporations. Globalization, particularly following events like China's entry into the World Trade Organization, allowed S&P 500 companies to offshore manufacturing and routine service jobs to lower-wage regions. This trend has recently faced headwinds, and many have argued that a reversal (aka de-globalization) process is underway. Such a reversal could offset some of the positives I mentioned above.

Second, the risk of persistently high inflation can also impact my above analyses—at least in indirect ways. In the ideal case where both revenues and profit are equally impacted by high inflation, there will be no change in profit margin. However, historical cases have demonstrated the possibility for inflation to impact different aspects of corporate financials to different degrees. These effects can change the cost structure (e.g., the share of labor, inputs, and/or financing costs) for corporations and in turn the profitability metrics.

To close, let me clarify that I am not trying to argue that the current equity market is cheap. The valuation multiples of all key indices are indeed elevated either in absolute terms or relative terms (e.g., when benchmarked by risk-free treasury rates). My goal is to argue that the P/E ratio itself only paints a partial picture. It overlooks other aspects such as the quality of the earnings and also the expansion potential. I see structural forces that keep sustaining the SP500’s robust profit margin or even further expanding it in the years to come, which should make equity valuation risks far lower than what the P/E ratios suggest.

data.title: Macquarie Large Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Equity indices once again reached record highs in 3Q25, with artificial intelligence (AI)-related stocks leading the market. Since early 2024, the S&P 500® Index has set 60 record highs, including new peaks in six of the nine months so far in 2025. AI stocks have been the primary driver, accounting for roughly 75% of market returns.

The third quarter saw new entrants into the AI ecosystem, notably Intel and Oracle, whose joint ventures and long-term deals propelled their stock prices higher. The Russell 1000® Growth Index rose more than 10%, an unusually strong quarterly gain that has only occurred 20 times over the past 20 years – 11 of those since the COVID-19 pandemic. The strongest-performing sectors were information technology (IT), communication services, and consumer discretionary.

Retail investors now account for record levels of daily trading volume, exerting significant influence on the market. Heavily shorted stocks performed well, defying long-term trends, as these stocks are typically shorted due to poor fundamentals. This signals that certain pockets of the market lack a solid foundation and that investors are increasingly willing to take on risk.

Market extremes have become more frequent and dramatic. Beta, a measure of volatility, has been a major driver of stock returns throughout the year. This means the most volatile stocks have significantly outperformed the broader market. Meanwhile, stocks with high-quality characteristics, such as attractive net margins and return on assets (ROA), have delivered mixed results. Interestingly, both the highest- and lowest-quality companies have generated strong returns. This reflects high-quality stocks benefiting from AI appreciating in value as well as a cohort of low-quality companies with poor fundamentals, likely heavily shorted by hedge funds and favored by retail investors.

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise noted.

Within the Fund

For 3Q25, Macquarie Large Cap Growth Fund (IYGIX) delivered positive returns but underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Growth Index.

An unfavorable market environment and several missteps drove the Fund’s underperformance. While the portfolio was positioned to benefit from the AI theme, we do not manage a thematic portfolio and aim to maintain a steady risk profile. As a result, we were unable to fully participate in this rally of extremes, favoring only the high-quality tail of AI exposures. Not fully appreciating the magnitude of market reactions to what we viewed as transitory issues in high-quality businesses hurt the Fund’s performance. We think this is unique to this risk-on environment. Holding certain positions too long despite having relatively lower conviction also detracted from performance. While we cannot undo these decisions, we are implementing measures to reduce the likelihood of similar occurrences in the future.

Sectorwise, stock selection in IT, financials, and consumer discretionary detracted most from the Fund’s performance, partially offset by positive selection in communication services.

At the stock level, the largest relative detractors were not owning Tesla (TSLA) and the Fund’s positions in Intercontinental Exchange Inc. (ICE) and Intuit Inc. (INTU). The Fund has never held Tesla as it does not meet our quality standards. It frequently emerges as either a top contributor or detractor each quarter, reflecting its volatile nature and the cycle of missed earnings and deferred expectations that has persisted for years. The company tends to miss expectations regularly, yet the market habitually buys shares based on fantastical multiyear aspirations.

ICE, which operates wide moat commodity exchanges, mainly futures and options for energy, as well as a mortgage technology business, fell out of favor during the quarter. Trading volumes slowed in several areas, particularly energy, which offset strength in equities. However, we focus on the long-term structural aspects of the energy market that favor sustained commodity volatility, supporting long-term volume growth. We believe ICE maintains a defendable, industry-leading position across multiple business lines with the potential for favorable long-term trends.

Intuit, operator of TurboTax and QuickBooks, faced several headwinds in 2025, though its business fundamentals remain solid. During the quarter, the stock declined when OpenAI announced a new, more advanced large language model, which the market perceived as a threat to application software broadly. We have not seen evidence that this poses a near- or medium-term threat to Intuit’s business; in fact, it may present new opportunities. Key growth drivers, such as the shift to take market share from the assisted tax preparation category and also penetration into the mid-market accounting market, are going extremely well despite downbeat market sentiment due to unfounded AI threats.

Contributing to relative performance were stocks in which the Fund holds an underweight position or no shares at all. Meta Platforms Inc. (META), parent company of Facebook, is a new but still underweight position in the portfolio. We recognize the company has quality characteristics – like its large user base and technology advantages – plus, not owning it presents risk management challenges relative to the benchmark given its significant benchmark weight. Nevertheless, we believe Meta still has valuation and quality hurdles to clear before becoming a full position, particularly providing more clarity on its use of capital.

Netflix Inc. (NFLX) is not held in the portfolio; while it is a good business, its quality level does not justify the current valuation in our opinion. This underweight benefited relative portfolio performance. Not owning Eli Lilly & Co. (LLY), a large pharmaceutical company with a GLP-1 franchise, also helped relative performance.

During the quarter, we made several changes to the portfolio. We initiated a position in Vulcan Materials Co. (VMC), one of two major aggregates companies in the US. Vulcan holds a dominant position in many regions, allowing for high market share and pricing power, which is exercised through annual price increases. With abundant infrastructure projects in the US, public contracts are accelerating and offsetting weakness in residential construction.

Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD), a US semiconductor company, was also added to the portfolio. The total addressable market for AI chips is vast and in need of a second supplier to complement NVIDIA. We believe AMD is now positioned to supply a competitive, and possibly superior, chip for inference on a price/performance basis. Additionally, its next-generation chip for AI training should be a viable option to supplement NVIDIA supply.

Equinix Inc. (EQIX), a data center operator, was eliminated from the portfolio. The company has unique assets in urban areas critical for reducing latency, especially as AI applications become more prevalent. However, as new locations open in second- and third-tier cities, Equinix has not been able to monetize these assets as we had expected. We believe there will be a better opportunity to own the stock in the future.

Outlook

There are many comparisons between the ongoing AI boom and the internet bubble, as well as notable differences. Massive amounts of capital are being spent, and hardware companies have been clear winners so far, leading to significant fixed asset investment and cash burn, all while the utility of products and applications are not yet fully understood. Among the many articles written during the internet bubble, a February 2000 Los Angeles Times story titled “A Trillion for Cisco, but Not a Dime for All Else?” discussed expectations among analysts that Cisco would be the first trillion-dollar company. Only a month after this article, technology stocks peaked and spent two years undergoing a reset.

We cannot predict the timing of a reset, nor do we expect the market to experience a period as tumultuous as the dot-com bubble. We are not questioning whether AI will be the next big thing; in many ways, it already is. However, many things remain to be figured out: business models, applications, regulation, power consumption, and hardware requirements, to name a few. It is an exciting time, and this portfolio has significant exposure to companies already benefiting, as well as others we believe will be future beneficiaries. However, we feel the market and many investors believe nothing can stop the AI juggernaut, allowing significant risk to build up. As long as the market continues to rise, we think these risks remain underappreciated.

Our quality-first approach has thrived through periods like this before, and we believe its merits are as relevant as ever. We will continue to maintain this discipline, providing our investors exposure to what we believe are the strongest growth businesses in the US - companies in dominant competitive positions that should thrive in both good and bad economic environments. As the market cycles, we believe our strategy will deliver significant benefits.

data.title: Magellan Aerospace: 'Strong Buy' On Aerospace Production Ramp Up
data.description:
Magellan Aerospace Corporation (OTCPK:MALJF, MAL:CA) is a materials and casting specialist for the aerospace and defense industry. I am bullish on the company, and that has paid off. Since my last report, the stock has gained 16%, and since I initiated coverage, the stock price has surged 88%. However, there are also some things that I do not like, which I will discuss in this report along with an analysis of the Q3 2025 earnings and an update to the price target.

Magellan Aerospace Provides Little Information To Investors

When it comes to the risk-reward profile, which I will also discuss in this report, I do like Magellan Aerospace. However, what I definitely do not like is the lack of insight provided. That already starts with even knowing when the company will report earnings. Magellan Aerospace does not provide a financial calendar on its website, and even with the Q3 results being published now, the results are not showing up on the company’s website at the time of writing, and the only way I know the company actually did report earnings is because I looked for it myself and found a press release through Businesswire. That is barely a good way to inform investors. Furthermore, the earnings release itself, as always, provides little color on the company’s performance by line of activity. The company reports by geographical segment, but that is not necessarily something that investors are interested in when trying to get an idea of what the company does. There is also no investor presentation, so Magellan Aerospace, to me, is an attractive name, but management and Investor Relations still have a lot to learn on how to properly attract and inform investors.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Magellan Aerospace?

Since Magellan Aerospace does not provide a lot of insight into the business, it may be useful to once again mention what it does:

Magellan Aerospace specializes in specialty materials, sand casting of aluminum and magnesium, allowing it to manufacture cold and hot sections for aero engines. The company also provides components and avionics for space and is active on key airplane programs, providing exhaust nozzles, winglet components, doors, and wing components.

While Magellan Aerospace does not provide an in-depth discussion of program and business line drivers within a certain quarter, we do know that with its product portfolio, it is positioned well to benefit from strong demand in the aerospace and defense industries.

The risks we see for the company are currency risks, as the company has sales and activities in Canada, Europe, and the United States. On the topic of tariffs, we note that Magellan Aerospace products are exempt from tariffs as they are aerospace components, and through USMCA compliance is being leveraged. However, the secondary impact of trade plays in the currency exchange rate sphere and could potentially be a headwind. The company’s growth path also hinges on airplane production ramp-ups. So, if the ramp-ups stall, that also gives Magellan Aerospace less growth space.

Q3 2025 Earnings Show Operating Leverage In Play

Revenues rose 14.4% to C$256.7 million, with gross profit increasing 30.3% to C$32.6 million, indicating gross margin expansion from 11.2% to 12.8%, while EBITDA increased 38.2% to C$29.8 million, indicating margin expansion from 9.6% to 11.6%.

In Canada, sales grew 14.9% to C$95.7 million. Revenue growth was driven by higher MRO activity and wide-body aircraft revenues. In the United States, sales grew 15.6% to C$73.3 million, driven by higher casting revenues, higher engine shaft revenues, and positive currency effects. On a constant currency basis, the growth would have been 14.4%. In Europe, sales grew 12.9%, driven by higher wide-body revenues, MRO, and currency exchange rates. On a constant currency basis, revenues would have increased 8.9%.

The growth was driven by higher casting revenues, as the comparable period casting revenues were negatively impacted by a work stoppage. In the United States, revenues grew by 1%, primarily driven by exchange rate fluctuations. On a constant currency basis, revenues would have been flat.

While Q2 earnings did not impress me, the Q3 results were strong, with double-digit sales growth and strong margin expansion driven by MRO demand and higher wide-body revenues. We also saw a strong improvement in margins. It does show that as the airplane production rate ticks up, the margins tick up rather quickly, driven by improving operating leverage.

Investment Case Remains Robust For Magellan Aerospace Stock

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Magellan Aerospace, the base case price target of $14.50 indicates 12% upside, with a $16.66 price target indicating 29% upside in a more optimistic scenario. For 2025, EBITDA estimates have been revised down by 3.8% but up 2.8% and 4.1% in the years after, indicating a total lift of 1.4%, while the compounded annual growth rate between 2024 and 2027 has improved from 17.2% to 18.8%. For free cash flow, there has been a total lift of 16.9% this year, little change next year, and 16.6% in the year after, for a total change of 10.9%, but we note that the timing of receipts may affect this year’s free cash flow on a heavily backloaded cash profile. The compounded annual growth rate rose from 5.5% to 11%.

Looking at the key metrics, what I like about Magellan Aerospace is the fact that margin performance is expected to improve substantially as operating leverage improves, with expectations of positive free cash flows being robust enough to drive net debt leverage to below one by the end of this year or possibly next year. So, the company is in a sound position as airplane production picks up, and my expectation is that with increasing commercial airplane build rates, we will continue to see significant step-ups in sales, margins, and cash flows.

Conclusion: Magellan Aerospace Remains Highly Attractive

Magellan Aerospace is one of the aerospace supply chain companies that has consistently shown appreciation in the stock price, and I believe that will remain that way, driven by improving airplane manufacturing volumes requiring more engine components, which Magellan Aerospace produces. The only thing I like a bit less about the company is that it provides extremely little detail on airplane program dynamics or any guidance that is useful to model expectations. Nonetheless, I maintain my "Strong Buy" rating for Magellan Aerospace stock.

data.title: Willis Lease Finance: This Name Now Trades At A 34% Discount To Adjusted Book Value (Upgrade).
data.description:
Investment thesis

When I previously covered Willis Lease Finance (WLFC), I rated the company a hold because the company was trading at a PB of 1.8x, which at the time I considered to be a fair valuation given the increasing demand for spare engines. Since my publication, the stock has declined by 23%, a shocking underperformance in an environment where most leasing stocks have trended higher. Prior to covering Willis, I covered AERCAP (AER) and Air Lease (AL) in one article, and since then both names have returned 21% and 13% in value, respectively. Now when we look at YTD performance, we see a wide dispersion between Willis and its peers. AER and AL both outperformed the market, which returned 17.56%; meanwhile, Willis lost 42% in value.

When we look at the multi-year historical performance, we can see that this name used to outperform both AER and AL, and it has only been recent that Willis began to underperform. I agree; for a short while Willis's valuation was stretched, and a correction needed to happen. Fortunately for us, it looks like the market has overly corrected, presenting a mispricing with a 34% upside. Hence, I'm upgrading this name to a strong buy.

Income Statement Analysis

Q3 2025

For the third quarter results, Willis's revenue increased 25% YoY to $183.39 million; this growth led to the beating of estimates by $27.39 million, bolstered by an 18% revenue increase from leasing, a 53% increase from maintenance reserves, and a 69% increase from gain on sales. This gain on sales was associated with a gross sale of $73.7 million in lease equipment, implying a margin on sale of 21.9%. The growth was offset by parts sales decreasing 50% to $5.39 million and maintenance service decreasing 39% to $3.64 million. Cost of revenue increased 4% to $40.48 million, resulting in gross profits growing 33% to $142.91 million and landing gross margins at 78%. Operating expenses increased by 48%, driven by a 23% increase in G&A, a 62% increase in technical expenses, and a $10.2 million asset write-down. Finance expenses increased by 34%, mainly driven by interest expense increasing to $34.18 million. The increase in OPEX and finance expenses resulted in operating income increasing 22% to $75.17 million and pretax income increasing 25% to $43.22 million, landing margins at 41% and 24%, respectively. Income tax increased by 82%, squeezing net income growth to $24.32 million, a 1% YoY increase, beating EPS estimates by $0.73.

9 Months 2025

When looking at the aggregate performance for all 3 quarters of 2025, revenue increased 29% to $536.62 million. The growth was driven by most segments of Willis's business except for maintenance service, which offset the growth by 4%. Management did state that most of its servicing facilities are operating at maximum capacity, and to combat that, they are currently developing a testing cell in Florida right near Pratt and Whitney factories, and they have recently opened a new aircraft maintenance hangar in Teesside. Asset write-downs totalled $23.77 million, resulting in OPEX increasing 57% to $193.20 million. It is concerning that despite a massive demand for spare engines, Willis is writing down the value of some of its assets. Management stated that these impaired engines are old, unserviceable, and ready to be monetized for sale. Finance expenses increased by 36%, resulting in pretax income increasing by 17%. It's worth noting that the $142.77 million pretax income was largely bolstered by $52.58 million from other income.

Operating income growing by just 1% is a tell that these massive increases in OPEX and finance expenses are starting to drag the margin expansion on the bottom line. Lease companies need to pay close attention to the interest coverage ratio, as their business model heavily relies on debt. If the debt is not managed well, it could massively impact the bottom line. The interest coverage ratio chart shows that even though interest expenses have been increasing, operating income is increasing faster. A decade ago the ratio was sitting at 1.42 and has now increased to 2.12. For reference, AER's ratio is sitting at 2.3 and AL's ratio is at 1.6. So even though I've flagged Willis's borrowing cost in my previous article, it looks like the company is not doing so bad on that end when compared to peers.

Cash Flow Statement Analysis

Q3 2025

When you look at Willis's profitability grading by the Seeking Alpha quant system, you will notice that it's graded a D-, and the reason for that is not earning margins; in fact, it's graded with an A+ on earnings margins. The problem is the free cash flow margin. AER has an FCF margin of -1%; the reason being, AER has been actively selling lease assets and using that cash to return value to shareholders. Willis's FCF margin is -39%, and the reason for that is that Willis is a small-cap that is actively trying to expand its market share in the leasing market; hence, it heavily commits all its operating cash flow to purchasing more leasing equipment. Its cash flow statement is illustrative of that move. In Q3 OCF decreased 6% to $63.87 million, which was caused by a negative working capital of $19.13 million despite D&A increasing by 21% and SBC increasing by 9%. Capital expenditure decreased 55% to $160.74 million, leading to a negative FCF decreasing 66% to $96.87 million.

9 Months 2025

For the 3 quarters of 2025, net income made a cash contribution of $101.58 million, and net working capital made a contribution of $6.45 million. Adding back another $101 million from non-cash items resulted in OCF decreasing 3% YoY to $209.08 million. Capital expenditure decreased by 32%, resulting in negative FCF of $124.72 million.

I would not advise investors to expect any inflection changes on the cash flow statement, as I believe this is a strategic move by the company to grow its market penetration. Despite negative FCF, the company continues to pay quarterly dividends; in fact, the company recently announced increasing its dividend payout by 60% to $0.40 per share.

Q3 Balance Sheet Analysis

When looking at Willis's balance sheet, we can see that the firm is using its financing channels to achieve 2 goals: firstly, to grow its asset base (reflected by a 12% increase in lease assets) and secondly, to extend the maturity of its debt (reflected by an 83% decrease in current debt). Long-term debt increased 37% to $2.17 billion, while restricted cash increased 59% to $158.1 million. Overall, total assets increased by 12%, and total liabilities increased by 10%, leading to shareholder's equity increasing 23% to $713.2 million and common equity increasing to $650.2 million.

Hidden Book value

Getting to the interesting part of this article, it is a known fact that in most cases asset leasing companies tend to normally trade at a premium/discount to market-adjusted book value, due to the assets held on the balance sheet not being regularly marked to market. Given the current demand dynamics in the aviation space, it was expected that leasing companies would trade at a discount to adjusted book value as the assets held appreciate in value. We normally measure this appreciation by the margin on sale the firm realizes from equipment gross sales. In 2024 Willis's margin on sale was 26% vs 16% in 2023, and in the last quarter the firm realized a margin on sale of 22%.

Earnings conversations below discuss how this asset appreciation has resulted in a value disparity of about $600 million compared to the stated book value.

When we account for this $600 million worth of hidden value to our stated book value, we get common equity of $1.25 billion. Given the current market cap of $820.56 million, we get a price-to-book ratio of 0.66. This means currently Willis trades at a 34% discount to book value.

On a comparative basis, we can see that Willis multiples have compressed to trade in line with AER. Looking at the growth numbers of Willis and AER and the fact that aircraft engines normally appreciate at a faster rate than airframes due to overhaul schedules, I believe Willis should be trading above AER.

Risk

Unless we are expecting a merger or an acquisition event, which we haven't heard any news of from management, it is not advisable, in my opinion, to heavily rely on price-book to value small-cap companies that are in their growth stage. The reason being, if there isn't going to be an acquisition or a liquidation event, we should value any company on its earnings power over time. Especially for Willis, management doesn't signal a tone of selling this company, and they are clearly aiming for growth; therefore, if no investor is paying attention to book value, the discount might not close. And looking at its earnings power, the growth of OPEX and interest expenses could contract margins going forward, leading to continued operating cash flow declines and persistent negative FCF. One further risk to pay attention to is the worthiness of the engine portfolio. If asset impairments begin to increase at an alarming rate, this could possibly mean that the engine portfolio is massively overvalued and it could be that price to book is at fair value.

Conclusion

Willis presents a good buying opportunity when we value its book value amid the appreciation of its engine portfolio. YTD the company has witnessed a 40% price drop, while peers and the broader market have given us double-digit returns. The title of my previous article was that all the tailwinds from the aviation dynamics have been priced in and since then the stock declined by 23%, including a 34% price-to-book discount I'm comfortable upgrading this name to a strong buy.

data.description:
This article updates my review published in February 2025 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

CDL strategy

VictoryShares US Large Cap High Div Volatility Wtd ETF (CDL) was launched on 07/08/2015 and tracks the Nasdaq Victory US Large Cap High Dividend 100 Volatility Weighted Index. CDL has a portfolio of 100 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 3.40%, and a net expense ratio of 0.35%. Distributions are paid monthly.

As described in the prospectus by Victory Capital, the methodology starts from the 500 largest U.S. companies by market capitalization with positive earnings. The 100 stocks with the highest dividend yields are included in the underlying index and weighted based on their volatility, measured as standard deviation of daily returns over the last 180 trading days. Stocks with lower volatility receive a higher weight, and stocks with higher volatility receive a lower weight, with a maximum of 25% by sector. The index is reconstituted semi-annually in March and September. The fund’s turnover rate was 44% in the most recent fiscal year and 38% in the previous year. Due to the starting universe, this article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 Index, represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

The portfolio has a focus on utilities (25.7%) and consumer staples (20.8%). Compared to the S&P 500, CDL massively overweights utilities, consumer staples, and energy, while it ignores real estate and downplays technology, communication, consumer discretionary, and industrials.
